_work.title: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
_work.creator: Abraham of Worms (attrib.); various
_work.blurb: The *Book of the Sacred Magic of Abramelin the Mage* (compiled c. 1458 by Abraham of Worms, preserved in BNF Arsenal MS 2351) ends with hundreds of talismanic *magic squares*: letter-grids where the rows, columns, and sometimes diagonals spell out the Hebrew names of spirits assigned to specific operations, from healing to treasure-finding to controlling kings. These word-squares — visually immediate, obsessively systematic — became the defining image of Abramelin magic after S. L. MacGregor Mathers translated the French manuscript in 1897, placing it at the heart of Golden Dawn and Thelemic practice. Here too is the wider magic-square tradition: Agrippa's seven planetary *kameas* from *De occulta philosophia* (1531), the Arabic *wafq* tradition of Al-Buni's *Shams al-Ma'arif*, and the SATOR-AREPO rotas square from Roman antiquity.

entries.title: SATOR-AREPO Word Square (Acrostic Diagram)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown (diagram from YDEA collection)
entries.date: Modern diagram; inscription attested from 1st c. CE
entries.rights: CC0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The SATOR-AREPO word square — five words, five letters each, arranged so they read identically left-to-right, right-to-left, top-to-bottom, and bottom-to-top — is the earliest and most durable magic word-square in the Western tradition.

## Iconography
The five words are **SATOR / AREPO / TENET / OPERA / ROTAS**, forming a perfect reversible grid. The central word TENET forms a cross; the whole can be rearranged into a double PATERNOSTER cross with the letters A and O (Alpha and Omega) left over. Whether the original meaning was Latin prose ('the sower Arepo holds the wheels at work') or a cryptic anagram is still debated, but the visual impact — a self-similar grid that reads the same in every direction — was clearly understood as a sign of hidden power.

## Significance
First attested at Pompeii (before 79 CE) and found across Roman military sites in Britain, Germany, and Egypt, the SATOR square entered medieval Christian amulet practice as a protection against fire, disease, and evil spirits. It is the direct ancestor of the Abramelin letter-square tradition: a grid of letters whose geometric perfection implies cosmic order and naming power. Scholars debate whether it originated in Jewish, early Christian, or Mithraic milieux; its ubiquity suggests a pan-cultural talismanic logic rather than a single origin.

## For artists & game designers
The SATOR square is the ur-puzzle of the Western tradition — self-referential, palindromic, and spatially complete. It works as an in-world cipher, a glyph on a cursed object, or the solution to a mystery that reveals who created a magical working.

entries.title: Rotas-Sator Amulet, Museu Monográfico de Conímbriga
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Roman craftsperson
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A physical survival: the SATOR-AREPO magic word-square engraved on an amulet-stone now in the Museu Monográfico de Conímbriga, Portugal — one of the most westerly Roman attestations of this ancient talisman.

## Iconography
The five-word square is incised in orderly Latin capitals on a flat stone. The medium itself — a portable inscribed object worn or carried — shows the square in its primary operational context: not an illustration but a tool. The grid reads ROTAS-OPERA-TENET-AREPO-SATOR in this presentation (the 'Rotas' variant), beginning with the word for 'wheels' rather than 'sower.'

## Significance
Conimbriga (near modern Coimbra) was a prosperous Roman city on the road from Olisipo (Lisbon) to Bracara Augusta. The amulet demonstrates that the magic square travelled with Roman soldiers, merchants, and settlers to the edges of the empire. Its presence in Portugal reinforces the theory that the square circulated within military and artisan communities rather than elite literary culture. The Museu Monográfico's collection of Roman amulets provides crucial material evidence for the lived practice of protective inscription magic.

## For artists & game designers
A graved stone inscribed with a word-square is the perfect prop for a historical RPG or dark fantasy setting — portable, mysterious, grammatically impossible to parse, and unmistakably the work of someone who believed letters themselves could hold power.

entries.title: Folio from Al-Buni's *Shams al-Ma'arif al-Kubra*, Khalili Collection
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Ahmad ibn Ali al-Buni (d. c. 1225); scribe unknown
entries.repository: Khalili Collection of Islamic Art, London
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 igo. Nasser D. Khalili Collection of Islamic Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A double-page opening from a copy of Ahmad ibn Ali al-Buni's *Shams al-Ma'arif al-Kubra* (The Great Sun of Knowledge, 13th c.) — the single most important work in the Islamic occult science of letter and number magic.

## Iconography
The folio spreads show the dense geometric layouts characteristic of al-Buni's opus: letter-grids (*wafq*, plural *awfaq*), numerical tables, and inscribed squares arranged amid Arabic text. These are not decorations but operative instruments — pages designed to be copied exactly, or the book itself consulted while performing a working. The grids combine the ninety-nine names of God (*asmā' al-ḥusnā*), abjad letter-number correspondences, and planetary hours into precise talismanic configurations.

## Significance
Al-Buni (d. c. 1225, in Tunis or Cairo) synthesised Arabic, Greek, and possibly Indian numerical traditions into a comprehensive theory of the *wafq* — the magic square as an instrument of divine power, not mere numerical curiosity. His *Shams al-Ma'arif* was the root from which the Islamic magic-square tradition branched across the Ottoman, Persian, Mughal, and Malay worlds. The European planetary kameas in Agrippa are ultimately indebted to this same Arabo-Hellenistic synthesis via the Picatrix and other transmission routes.

## For artists & game designers
The densely packed letter-grids of al-Buni offer an aesthetic unavailable in European grimoire art: Arabic script in talismanic geometry, angular and cursive by turns, with a visual logic that game artists could adapt for any 'Arabic' or 'Islamicate' magic system.

entries.title: Folio from Al-Buni's Treatise on the Ninety-Nine Names of God, Khalili Collection
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Ahmad ibn Ali al-Buni (d. c. 1225); scribe unknown
entries.repository: Khalili Collection of Islamic Art, London
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 igo. Nasser D. Khalili Collection of Islamic Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A double-page spread from Al-Buni's treatise specifically on the *Asmā' Allāh al-Ḥusnā* (Ninety-Nine Names of God), one of the most copied texts in the Islamic occult corpus, here from a Khalili Collection manuscript.

## Iconography
The opening displays letter-grids and numerical tables keyed to the divine names. Each name of God in the Islamic tradition has a numerical value (via the *abjad* system, where each Arabic letter corresponds to a number) and a specific set of properties — for health, victory, protection, sustaining life, or defeating enemies. The grids here encode these properties into geometric forms suitable for transfer to amulets and talismans.

## Significance
The *wafq* system built around the ninety-nine names became one of the most widespread talismanic practices in the Islamic world, crossing sectarian lines and geographical boundaries. A *wafq* containing the letters of a divine name, inscribed on paper or metal and worn on the body, was believed to transmit that attribute of God directly to the bearer. This is structurally identical to the Abramelin method, where the letters of a spirit's name, inscribed in a word-square, confer that spirit's powers. The parallel — likely not coincidental given the medieval Hebrew-Arabic intellectual exchange — was noted by scholars including Moshe Idel.

## For artists & game designers
Letter-squares built from divine names provide a morally complex magic system: the talisman-maker is not commanding spirits but invoking divine attributes — making the magic prayerful rather than coercive, devotional rather than commanding.

entries.title: Double-Sided Polygonal Talisman, Khalili Collection
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Islamic craftsperson
entries.repository: Khalili Collection of Islamic Art, London
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 igo. Nasser D. Khalili Collection of Islamic Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A double-sided polygonal metal talisman from the Khalili Collection — a physical object of protective and operative magic produced in the Ottoman or Qajar Islamic world, engraved on all faces with letter-grids and divine names.

## Iconography
The talisman's polygonal form (likely octagonal or twelve-sided) provides multiple faces for inscription, each panel carrying a different *wafq* grid or invocation. Metal talismans of this type were typically produced by specialist craftsmen working from recipe books like al-Buni's *Shams al-Ma'arif*: the client specified the operation (protection from enemies, success in business, safe travel), and the craftsman selected the appropriate divine names, numerical squares, and planetary correspondences, then engraved them under the correct astrological hours.

## Significance
The polygonal metal talisman is the highest-grade material form of the *wafq* tradition — more durable than paper, more complex than cloth, and requiring specialist engraving skill. Such objects circulated at courts and in wealthy households across the Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal worlds. Their existence in museum collections (Khalili, V&A, Topkapi) demonstrates that the magic-square talisman was not a marginal practice but central to early modern Islamic material culture.

## For artists & game designers
A multi-faced engraved metal talisman is an ideal prop for an Islamicate fantasy setting — it implies a structured magical technology, craftsperson traditions, and an economy of protective devices comparable to a pharmaceutical industry.

entries.title: 16th-Century Arabic Magic Square
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A compact manuscript page bearing a single Arabic magic square (*wafq*) — a concentrated example of the Islamic talismanic numerical tradition in its mature 16th-century form.

## Iconography
The grid is divided into cells each containing an Arabic numeral or letter. The rows, columns, and diagonals sum to the same constant — the 'magic constant' that marks the square as formally correct and therefore operationally valid. Surrounding text probably identifies the square by the name of God or the operation it performs. The script and layout suggest a practitioner's manual rather than a luxury presentation copy: a working document.

## Significance
The 16th century was the great age of *wafq* compilation in the Arabic-speaking world, as authors built on al-Buni's 13th-century foundations and absorbed Indian and Persian traditions of numerical magic. By this period every town with a literate population would have had access to *wafq* recipe books: printed or copied lists of squares keyed to specific human needs. The visual difference between this Islamic square and a European planetary kamea is primarily one of script and labelling convention; the underlying mathematical structure is identical.

## For artists & game designers
A single tightly ruled grid on yellowed paper, with each cell containing a numeral — the minimal expression of talismanic power. Works as a found-object clue, a magical component, or a page from a captured enemy wizard's codex.

entries.title: Buduh Magic Square (Arabic Amulet Diagram)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown
entries.date: Modern diagram; tradition attested from medieval period
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The *Buduh* square — the Arabic 3×3 magic square with the specific letter values **ب-د-و-ح** (ba, dal, waw, ha), corresponding to the numerals 2-4-6-8 in the *abjad* system — is one of the most widespread single talismanic objects in the Islamic world.

## Iconography
This small diagram shows the 3×3 grid containing the letters of *buduh* distributed so that each row, column, and diagonal sums to the same value. The name *buduh* itself has no independent meaning in Arabic; it is a technical term coined from the letter-values of this specific arrangement, attested in al-Buni and widely reproduced in talismanic manuals ever since.

## Significance
The *buduh* square was used across North Africa, the Near East, and into South and Southeast Asia as a general-purpose protective talisman — worn as an amulet, inscribed on doorposts, copied into personal prayer books. It is the Islamic analogue of the SATOR square in the Western tradition: a minimal letter-grid of such wide diffusion that its origin is untraceable, its meaning largely symbolic and protective rather than specific to any one operation. Its extraordinary longevity — attested from the 13th century to the present — demonstrates the deep human appetite for the talismanic letter-grid.

## For artists & game designers
The *buduh* square is a worldbuilding detail of high specificity: a 3×3 letter-grid that any character in an Islamicate setting would recognise immediately, worn like a cross in a Christian world — private piety made portable.

entries.title: Magic Square from a Chetveroevangelie (Orthodox Gospel Book)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Orthodox scribe
entries.medium: manuscript illumination, ink and pigment on parchment
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A magic square incorporated into the decorative programme of an Eastern Orthodox *Chetveroevangelie* (Gospel Tetraevangelion) — a rare witness to the penetration of the magic-square tradition into Christian liturgical manuscript production.

## Iconography
The square appears amid the ornamented frames and canon tables characteristic of Slavic Gospel manuscripts. Its incorporation here is not incidental decoration: in Eastern Christian tradition, geometric grids with numerical or alphabetic properties were understood as expressions of divine order, akin to the Solomonic geometries of the Western grimoire tradition. The square's letters or numbers would have been read as sacred numerology reinforcing the text's authority.

## Significance
The Eastern Orthodox encounter with magic-square tradition was mediated differently from the Western Latin one: through Byzantine Greek sources, through Jewish communities in the Slavic lands, and through the same Arabic-Greek transmission that produced the Picatrix. The presence of a magic square in a Gospel book — the most sacred text in the Orthodox liturgical corpus — shows how thoroughly the talismanic grid had been naturalised into Christian devotional culture. This convergence makes the magic square one of the most cross-cultural artifacts in the pre-modern world.

## For artists & game designers
A magic square embedded in a holy book inverts the usual occult-vs-religion binary: here sacred text and talismanic number-grid are presented as complementary expressions of the same divine order.

entries.title: Hebrew Manuscript Amulet for Protection from Plague (Wellcome Collection)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Jewish scribe
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An Ashkenazi Hebrew manuscript amulet for the protection of a named individual from plague — a personal talismanic document combining kabbalistic letter-magic with Psalm verses and divine names in the tradition that directly parallels the Abramelin word-squares.

## Iconography
The amulet is a sheet of paper inscribed in Hebrew with a combination of protective Psalm verses, divine names, and letter-grids or *kamea*-style arrangements. The name of the protected person is typically written into the formula ('for the protection of [name], son/daughter of [mother's name]'), personalising the talisman. The script is Ashkenazi square Hebrew with some cursive elements, suggesting a scribe who was a literate community specialist — possibly the local rabbi or a professional amulet-writer.

## Significance
The Wellcome Collection holds one of the largest holdings of Hebrew protective amulets in the world, acquired during the 19th and early 20th centuries when such documents were sold or donated from European Jewish communities. They document the living practice of Hebrew letter-magic — the same tradition that informs the Abramelin word-squares — in its everyday rather than grimoire form: not elite ritual magic but domestic protective practice, handwritten and personalised for ordinary people facing epidemic disease.

## For artists & game designers
A personalised plague amulet with a specific name written in Hebrew is a powerful narrative prop: it belongs to someone, it was made with urgency, and the person who commissioned it believed their life depended on the correct arrangement of letters on a page.

entries.title: Kabbalistic Amulet for the Cure from Jaundice, 18th c. (Wellcome Collection)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Jewish scribe
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A kabbalistic healing amulet written for Abraham Elijah son of a named mother, prescribing a cure from jaundice through the operative use of divine names and letter-grids — an 18th-century Ashkenazi document of vernacular Jewish magic.

## Iconography
The amulet combines Psalm fragments, angelic names (likely Raphael, the healing angel), divine names from the kabbalistic tradition, and potentially a small letter-grid *kamea* keyed to the healing operation. The combination of biblical text and magical formula is characteristic of the *kame'ot* (amulet) tradition: Scripture provides the authoritative matrix; the divine names and geometric arrangements are the operational technology layered within it.

## Significance
This amulet documents the interface between kabbalistic theory (the emanative power of divine names, the correspondence between letters and aspects of the divine) and folk medical practice. Jaundice was a serious condition in early modern Europe with limited effective treatment; the amulet was part of a therapeutic package that might also include herbal remedies and prayer. The Wellcome Collection's holdings from this tradition form a documentary archive of how kabbalistic ideas, ultimately sharing roots with the Abramelin letter-magic, were operationalised at the level of everyday life.

## For artists & game designers
A healing amulet written for a specific person, specifying the illness and the beneficiary's name, is the most intimate possible magical object. It implies a community of practice, a working relationship between scribe and patient, and a worldview in which the correct letters, correctly arranged, could alter the body's condition.

entries.title: Kabbalistic Amulet for Safe Pregnancy and Delivery, 18th c. (Wellcome Collection)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Jewish scribe
entries.rights: CC BY 4.0. Wellcome Collection. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A kabbalistic amulet for the safe pregnancy and delivery of Esther wife of a named husband — a standard genre of Ashkenazi protective magic, among the most commonly produced amulets in the Jewish world, here from the Wellcome Collection's 18th-century holdings.

## Iconography
Birth amulets typically invoke the angels Sanoi, Sansanoi, and Semangelaf (the three angels sent to retrieve Lilith from the sea in medieval legend) alongside Psalm 121 ('He who watches over Israel shall neither slumber nor sleep') and other texts. They commonly include letter-grids or magical names arranged in geometric patterns, and the name of the pregnant woman written explicitly into the formula. Some include illustrations of the angels, the menorah, or other iconic forms.

## Significance
The birth amulet (*kame'a* for *leidah*, for birth) is the most extensively documented type of Jewish protective talisman. Childbed was the most dangerous regular experience in a woman's pre-modern life; the amulet offered a form of spiritual protection that coexisted with midwifery skill. The invocation of Lilith's enemies (the three angels) in protective charms is a striking example of how apotropaic magic uses the names of threatening supernatural forces as deterrents. This is structurally identical to the Abramelin method: the names of spirits, correctly inscribed, control those spirits.

## For artists & game designers
A pregnancy protection amulet is a morally complex magic item: protective, not aggressive; gendered; tied to the most vulnerable moment of domestic life. It makes visible the magic system's social function — not power-seeking but care.

entries.title: Islamic Magic Bowl (Khalili Collection)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Islamic craftsperson
entries.repository: Khalili Collection of Islamic Art, London
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 3.0 igo. Nasser D. Khalili Collection of Islamic Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An engraved metal magic bowl from the Khalili Collection — a material object of operative Islamic magic, inscribed with divine names and *wafq* grids for healing or protective use through the medium of water consumed from the vessel.

## Iconography
The bowl's inner surface is covered with Arabic inscriptions: divine names, Quranic verses (particularly al-Fatiha and Ayat al-Kursi, the 'Throne Verse'), and compact numerical or letter-grids. The outer surface may carry further protective inscriptions. These bowls were used for healing: water, milk, or honey was poured in, allowed to absorb the inscribed power, then given to the patient to drink. The bowl itself could also be pressed against the body at the site of illness.

## Significance
Islamic medicine bowls (*kasa*) represent the intersection of the *wafq* tradition, Quranic healing practice, and material culture. They were produced throughout the Islamic world — Iran, Ottoman Anatolia, South Asia — and their production required both scribal literacy and metalworking skill. The practice of ingesting or touching a surface inscribed with divine names is directly analogous to the Hermetic idea (found in the Leyden Papyrus and in early Christian amulet practice) that sacred letters have physical as well as spiritual effects.

## For artists & game designers
A magic bowl that must be drunk from — where the power is transmitted through the inscription via liquid — is a striking mechanic. The magic is not worn, it is consumed; the letter-grid enters the body.

entries.title: Egyptian Ink-on-Paper Amulet, LACMA (M.2002.1.370)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Egyptian craftsperson
entries.repository: Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA)
entries.rights: Public domain. Los Angeles County Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A Fatimid or Ayyubid-period Egyptian ink-on-paper amulet from LACMA's collection — one of the oldest surviving examples of the Islamic talismanic document tradition, predating al-Buni's systematisation but already displaying the letter-grid aesthetic.

## Iconography
The amulet is a piece of paper inscribed with Arabic script in a compact, grid-like arrangement — magical names, protective formulae, and possibly a numerical or letter square. Egyptian paper amulets of this period were folded and worn inside small leather cases suspended from the body. The ink-on-paper format made amulet production accessible: any literate person with access to the necessary texts could produce one.

## Significance
Fatimid Egypt (909–1171 CE) was a crucible of Ismaili esoteric thought and a centre of the letter-magic tradition that al-Buni would systematise a century later. These early amulets document the pre-al-Buni state of Islamic protective writing: already using divine names, Quranic verses, and geometric arrangements, but not yet with the elaborate numerical *wafq* grids that would later dominate. LACMA's collection of such objects is an important resource for tracing the evolution of Islamic talismanic practice.

## For artists & game designers
A small, folded, ink-on-paper amulet worn inside a leather case against the skin is the most intimate portable magic: invisible, personal, and constant. The idea that a piece of paper with the right words on it protects the wearer's life inverts the modern secular assumption that writing is merely communication.

entries.title: Egyptian Ink-on-Paper Amulet, LACMA (M.2002.1.371)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Egyptian craftsperson
entries.repository: Los Angeles County Museum of Art (LACMA)
entries.rights: Public domain. Los Angeles County Museum of Art. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A second Egyptian ink-on-paper amulet from LACMA's collection — a narrow scroll-format document inscribed with protective formulae, part of the same Fatimid-Ayyubid talismanic tradition but in a different physical format from the folded-sheet type.

## Iconography
This amulet is in scroll format: a long narrow strip of paper densely covered in small Arabic script arranged in columns and rows. Scroll amulets were typically worn rolled and inserted into cylindrical silver or copper cases (*hifaza*) suspended from a chain or cord. The narrow format concentrates the inscribed power into a portable object that can be worn against the heart or the upper arm.

## Significance
The scroll and the folded-sheet are the two principal formats for Egyptian Islamic paper amulets. Both formats were already in use by the Fatimid period, suggesting a well-established production tradition with workshop conventions. The scroll format has a long history in the ancient Egyptian magic tradition (hieratic papyrus amulets from the Ptolemaic period are its ancestors); the Islamic *hifaza* represents the continuity of this Egyptian talismanic practice through the Islamic period, absorbing new content (Quranic verses, Arabic divine names) while retaining the old material form.

## For artists & game designers
A rolled scroll carried in a silver case at the chest, its contents unknown to anyone but the person who commissioned and the scribe who wrote it — the most private form of magic, combining body, metal, and inscribed language in a single wearable object.

entries.title: Lo Shu Magic Square (Chinese Mystic Table, from Paul Carus)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Paul Carus (ill.); traditional Chinese pattern
entries.date: 1906 (from *The Monist*); tradition attested from Han dynasty
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The Lo Shu (*Lò shū*, 洛書, 'Luo River Script') magic square — the 3×3 numerical grid whose rows, columns, and diagonals all sum to 15 — as illustrated by philosopher Paul Carus in a 1906 article for *The Monist*, demonstrating the square's cross-cultural significance.

## Iconography
The diagram presents the Lo Shu in its 'mystic knot' form: dots and lines (the traditional Chinese representation) on the left, and the corresponding Arabic numerals arranged in the 3×3 grid on the right. The same numerical arrangement appears in Chinese tradition as the map carried on the back of the cosmic tortoise that emerged from the Luo River, granting the legendary Emperor Yu the knowledge needed to order the universe.

## Significance
The Lo Shu is the 3×3 magic square — the smallest possible magic square with distinct positive integers — and it appears independently in Chinese mathematics (attested from the Han dynasty, 206 BCE–220 CE) and in the Arabic *wafq* tradition (where it is the *buduh* square or the Saturn kamea), and possibly in Indian Jain mathematics. Whether these represent independent discovery or transmission along trade routes (Silk Road, Indian Ocean) is debated. For the history of magic squares, the Lo Shu is ground zero: the mother-pattern from which all larger squares — including the 4×4 Jupiter kamea and the vast squares of the Abramelin — descend.

## For artists & game designers
The Lo Shu establishes that magic squares are not a Western or Islamic peculiarity but a near-universal human discovery. In a multicultural fantasy world, the fact that widely separated civilisations arrived at the same grid independently could serve as evidence of a shared underlying metaphysical reality.

entries.title: Three Magical Alphabets from Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia*
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa von Nettesheim
entries.date: 1531 (first ed.); plate here from a later facsimile
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The three magical alphabets from Book III of Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia* (1531/1533) — the Celestial Script, the Malachim script, and the Passing of the River script — which provided the Renaissance occult tradition with its definitive system of encoded magical writing.

## Iconography
Three complete alphabets are laid out in parallel rows, with the standard Latin letters on one axis and their equivalents in each magical script across from them. The Celestial Script (*scriptura caelestis*) uses forms derived from constellations; the Malachim ('of the angels') uses forms based on a rationalisation of square Hebrew; the Passing of the River (*transitus fluvii*) is a further variant. These are the scripts in which, Agrippa argues, divine names and magical formulae can be written with particular efficacy.

## Significance
Agrippa's three alphabets became the standard reference for Renaissance and early modern occultists writing spirit-seals, talisman inscriptions, and magic circle letters. They appear in the Goetia (where the Theban alphabet, derived from these, is attributed to Honorius), in John Dee's angelic records, and in countless manuscript and printed grimoires. Their visual logic is closely related to the Abramelin letter-squares: both assume that the visible form of a letter is not arbitrary but carries intrinsic power, and that the power can be concentrated or encoded by using the 'true' form of the letter rather than its everyday equivalent.

## For artists & game designers
Three distinct magical scripts with full alphabets — a complete worldbuilding kit. Each script has a different aesthetic register: astronomical, angelic, riverine. A world-builder can assign each to a different tradition or period.

entries.title: Human Proportions and Secret Numbers, *De Occulta Philosophia*
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa von Nettesheim
entries.date: 1531 (first ed.); plate here from 1533 Cologne edition
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The famous human-proportions diagram from Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia* Book II — a man inscribed in a circle, arms and legs outstretched, with numerical annotations marking the sacred ratios of the human body, cousin to Leonardo's Vitruvian Man.

## Iconography
A standing human figure with arms spread horizontally and legs at shoulder width is enclosed in a circle. Numbers annotate the body's proportions according to ancient and Pythagorean-Neoplatonist number theory: the distance from crown to navel to foot, the span of the arms, the intervals of the face. The implicit claim is that the human body encodes the same mathematical relationships as the planetary kameas — that man is a microcosm whose proportions mirror cosmic numerical order.

## Significance
This diagram sits at the intersection of the Renaissance body-as-microcosm theory and the magic-square tradition. For Agrippa, the planetary kameas are not merely arithmetical curiosities but numerical expressions of the planetary spheres — and those spheres are mapped onto the human body via its sacred proportions. The magic square is thus a mediator between the macrocosm (planets) and the microcosm (human body): an image that captures a cosmic ratio and allows it to be inscribed on a talisman. This diagram makes that theoretical connection visible.

## For artists & game designers
A man inscribed in a circle with numbers marking his proportions is the most enduring image of the Renaissance magical worldview: the universe is mathematical; the body is the universe in miniature; and whoever knows the numbers knows the key to both.

entries.title: Illustration from Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia* (1651 edition)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa von Nettesheim
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
An illustration from the 1651 English-language edition of Agrippa's *De Occulta Philosophia* (translated by J.F. and published in London) — a page from the most influential single compendium of Renaissance magical theory.

## Iconography
The 1651 edition's illustrations include magical figures, signs, and geometric diagrams derived from the 1531/1533 Latin originals. This particular plate (designated S3 in the Wikimedia Commons filing) belongs to Agrippa's system of magical characters — forms derived from letters, numbers, and celestial configurations that could be used as spirit-seals or inscribed on talismans.

## Significance
The 1651 English translation brought Agrippa to a new readership outside the Latin-literate scholarly world: it was read by cunning-folk, astrologers, and the growing literate urban class of Interregnum England. Francis Barrett's *The Magus* (1801) drew heavily on it; so did the grimoire tradition that would eventually produce the published Key of Solomon texts of the 19th century. The magic-square kameas that appear in Agrippa's Book II sit within this vast systematic framework: number, letter, planet, spirit, character, and talisman are all faces of the same underlying occult philosophy.

## For artists & game designers
A small engraving of a magical character or sign from Agrippa is the seed of an entire magic system: behind each image stands a planetary correspondence, a spirit name, a numerical value, and a human purpose.

entries.title: 17th-Century Picatrix Frontispiece, Hagia Sophia MS 2443
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown Ottoman scribe
entries.date: 17th c. (copy of Arabic original, *Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm*, c. 11th c.)
entries.rights: Public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The frontispiece of a 17th-century Ottoman copy of the *Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm* (*The Goal of the Wise*, known in Latin as the *Picatrix*) — the major Arabic text of astral magic and the key intermediary through which planetary kameas, magic squares, and spirit-images were transmitted from the Islamicate world to Renaissance Europe.

## Iconography
The frontispiece page bears the title of the work in ornate Arabic script, with decorative borders appropriate to a prestige copy. The *Ghāyat al-Ḥakīm* (compiled c. 11th century CE, probably in Andalusia) is a massive encyclopaedia of astral magic: how to attract planetary influences, manufacture astral talismans, and command spirits through the correct use of planetary images, suffumigations, prayers, and numeric grids.

## Significance
The *Picatrix* is the bridge between the Islamic *wafq* tradition and the European planetary-magic tradition. It was translated into Castilian and then Latin for Alfonso X of Castile in the 13th century, and from there circulated through European scholarly networks to reach Ficino, Agrippa, and ultimately the Renaissance Hermetic tradition. Agrippa's planetary kameas in *De Occulta Philosophia* derive their mathematical structures from material in the *Picatrix* milieu; the planetary images and spirit-names also trace back to it. Without the *Picatrix*, the Abramelin magic-square tradition would have no European grounding.

## For artists & game designers
The *Picatrix* is the sourcebook of sourcebooks: a magical encyclopaedia so comprehensive and systematic that it provided the building-blocks for European ceremonial magic for five centuries. A copy of it in a fantasy world would be the most dangerous library acquisition imaginable.

entries.title: Post-Medieval Copper Alloy Talismanic Pendant (FindID 755270)
entries.work: The Book of Abramelin & the Magic Square Tradition
entries.creator: Unknown craftsperson
entries.rights: CC BY 2.0. Portable Antiquities Scheme. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A copper alloy talismanic pendant recovered through the UK Portable Antiquities Scheme — a physical survival of post-medieval English amulet practice, likely bearing magic squares or other protective inscriptions derived from the Renaissance grimoire tradition.

## Iconography
The pendant is a cast copper alloy medallion with engraved inscriptions on one or both faces. Post-medieval copper talismans of this type typically bear one or more of: the planetary kameas derived from Agrippa; fragments of Psalm text; divine names (IAO, AGLA, Tetragrammaton); magical characters or spirit-seals from the grimoire tradition; or simple geometric protective patterns. The copper medium and size suggest a wearable object intended for personal rather than domestic use.

## Significance
The Portable Antiquities Scheme has recorded hundreds of post-medieval talismanic pendants across England, demonstrating the wide circulation of grimoire-derived amulet practice outside elite manuscript culture. By the 16th–18th centuries, the planetary kameas and magical characters of Agrippa had been absorbed into vernacular protective magic practised at all social levels. A copper pendant sold or made locally and worn against the skin is the low-cost democratic version of the elaborate ritual magic documented in manuscripts like the Abramelin: the same underlying technology of letters, numbers, and divine names, packaged for everyday accessibility.

## For artists & game designers
A found metal pendant with obscure engraved symbols — the kind of thing pulled from an English field by a metal detectorist — is a perfect archaeological mystery object. What is it for? Whose power does it invoke? The answer connects to five centuries of grimoire tradition.
